article_title: Intercom’s npm Package Compromised in Ongoing Mini Shai-Hulud Worm Attack
core_thesis: The widely used npm package intercom-client@7.0.4 was compromised in a supply chain attack linked to the ongoing Mini Shai-Hulud worm campaign, which targets developer and CI/CD environments to steal Kubernetes, Vault, and cloud credentials via malicious preinstall scripts and obfuscated payloads, exfiltrating data through GitHub API.
detailed_summary: Socket's Threat Research team detected and confirmed that version 7.0.4 of intercom-client, an official Node.js SDK for Intercom's API with ~360,000 weekly downloads and over 100 dependent projects, contains two malicious files not present in the previous version: setup.mjs and router_runtime.js. The package includes a preinstall hook that executes setup.mjs, which downloads and runs an unverified Bun binary from GitHub without integrity checks. The second file, router_runtime.js (11.7 MB, heavily obfuscated), harvests credentials from environment variables and local files—targeting Kubernetes, Vault, cloud providers (AWS, GCP), and GitHub tokens—then encrypts and exfiltrates them via the GitHub API. The attack mirrors the lightning@2.6.2 PyPI attack and the TeamPCP-linked SAP CAP campaign, sharing identical payload patterns, GitHub-based exfiltration, and targeting of CI/CD pipelines. The compromise originated from a compromised GitHub account (nhur) that created suspicious repositories, pushed malicious workflow changes to 11 repositories in the intercom organization, and triggered an automated CI publish workflow to release the malicious package. The malicious code runs during installation, so even systems that never imported the package are at risk. Indicators of compromise include file hashes, public RSA keys, and network indicators for AWS and GCP metadata endpoints.

entities.name: Intercom
entities.role: Vendor of the compromised npm package

entities.name: nhur
entities.role: Compromised GitHub account used to publish malicious package and modify repositories

entities.name: TeamPCP
entities.role: Threat group linked to similar supply chain attacks

entities.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
entities.role: Ongoing supply chain worm campaign targeting developer environments

key_data_points: intercom-client@7.0.4 has ~360,000 weekly downloads and over 100 dependent projects, indicating a broad potential impact (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: high-value target for supply chain compromise).

The malicious preinstall script (setup.mjs) downloads and executes an unverified Bun binary from GitHub without integrity checks, enabling arbitrary code execution during npm install (🟢 Red Teaming: this technique can be weaponized for initial access in CI/CD pipelines).

router_runtime.js (11.7 MB, obfuscated) harvests Kubernetes, Vault, AWS, GCP, and GitHub credentials from environment variables and local files, then exfiltrates via GitHub API (🟢 Red Teaming: demonstrates credential harvesting and exfiltration methods useful for post-exploitation).

The attack shares technical overlap with the lightning@2.6.2 PyPI attack and TeamPCP-linked SAP CAP campaign, indicating a coordinated or copycat campaign (🟢 Red Teaming: understanding campaign TTPs aids in threat intelligence and detection).

The compromised GitHub account (nhur) created branches mimicking Dependabot (e.g., 'dependabout/github_actions/...') and pushed workflows that exfiltrate repository secrets via GitHub Actions artifacts (🟢 Red Teaming: social engineering and CI/CD abuse techniques for persistence).

Network indicators include AWS IMDS (169.254.169.254), ECS endpoints, and GCP metadata (metadata.google.internal), showing targeting of cloud metadata services (🟢 Red Teaming: common technique for cloud credential theft).
methodology_steps: 1. Detection: Socket AI scanner flagged intercom-client@7.0.4 as malicious; manual analysis confirmed two new files (setup.mjs, router_runtime.js) not in version 7.0.3.
2. Static analysis: Identified preinstall hook in package.json that executes setup.mjs during npm install.
3. Dynamic analysis: setup.mjs downloads a Bun binary from a GitHub release (no integrity check) and executes it with an embedded JavaScript payload.

4. Payload analysis: router_runtime.js (11.7 MB, obfuscated) scans environment variables and local files for credentials (Kubernetes, Vault, AWS, GCP, GitHub tokens), encrypts them, and sends via GitHub API.
5. Attribution: Compared with lightning@2.6.2 PyPI attack and TeamPCP-linked SAP CAP campaign; found identical payload structure and exfiltration methods.
6. GitHub investigation: Traced malicious publish to compromised account nhur, which created suspicious repos, pushed workflow changes to 11 intercom repos, and triggered CI publish workflow.
7. IOC collection: Extracted file hashes (SHA256, SHA1, MD5), public RSA keys, and network indicators (AWS/GCP metadata endpoints).
conclusions: The intercom-client compromise is part of a broader supply chain campaign (Mini Shai-Hulud) that uses similar techniques across npm, PyPI, and Packagist ecosystems, emphasizing the need for multi-ecosystem monitoring (🟢 Red Teaming: cross-ecosystem TTPs can be leveraged for broader attacks).

The attack exploits trust in CI/CD automation by mimicking Dependabot branches and using compromised accounts to push malicious workflows, highlighting the importance of verifying CI triggers and branch protections (🟢 DevOps: implement branch protection rules and verify commit signatures).

The malicious code executes during installation, meaning even indirect dependencies or unused packages can compromise environments; this underscores the need for runtime detection and dependency scanning (🟢 Homelab: use tools like Socket or npm audit to block malicious packages before installation).

Credential harvesting targets Kubernetes, Vault, and cloud metadata services, which are common in modern infrastructure; rotating secrets and restricting metadata access can mitigate impact (🟢 Red Teaming: these are high-value targets for post-exploitation).
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming and DevOps interests as it details a real-world supply chain attack that weaponizes npm packages to steal credentials from CI/CD pipelines and cloud environments. You can learn the specific TTPs—preinstall hooks, Bun binary execution, obfuscated credential harvesters, and GitHub API exfiltration—to simulate similar attacks in your own red team exercises. For your homelab and DevOps work, the article provides concrete indicators of compromise (file hashes, network endpoints) and mitigation steps (downgrade packages, rotate secrets, review CI workflows). As a programmer, understanding how malicious code is injected into popular SDKs helps you write more secure dependency management practices. The cross-ecosystem nature (npm, PyPI, Packagist) also broadens your threat modeling scope.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a honeypot environment that mimics a CI/CD pipeline with fake credentials (e.g., Vault tokens, AWS keys) and install the malicious package in a sandbox to observe its behavior firsthand. This will deepen your understanding of the attack chain and help you develop custom detection rules for your own infrastructure. Additionally, review your own CI/CD workflows for any branches or actions that mimic Dependabot or use unverified third-party actions, and enforce commit signing and branch protection rules.
